plans.name: Automotive Battery Inspection, Handling, and Sorting
plans.content:
Automotive Battery Inspection, Handling, and Sorting

1. Title
Human–Robot Collaboration for Automotive Battery Inspection and Sorting

2. Target Audience
• Entry-level or vocational students

3. Learning Objectives
• Understand battery types (lead-acid, AGM, EV lithium packs)
• Identify hazardous conditions on batteries
• Practice safe human–robot collaboration (HRC)
• Handle heavy batteries with a collaborative robot
• Perform sustainability evaluation
• Make sorting decisions: Recycle, Dismantle, Discard
• Complete a full integrated workflow

4. Scenario Overview
A continuous XR scenario simulates an industrial environment where humans and robots collaborate to inspect, handle, and sort heavy automotive batteries. The learner progresses from basic safety procedures to advanced decision-making and robot-assisted sorting.

5. Pedagogical Approach
• Stepwise guided learning
• Learning-by-doing with HRC emphasis
• Progressive difficulty and diminishing guidance
• Immediate feedback from the virtual assistant
• Automatic safety monitoring and correction
• End-to-end workflow assessment

6. Scenario Structure
Phase 1: Spawn and Orientation
Phase 2: Human–Robot Collaboration Safety Zones
Phase 3: Battery Types Exploration
Phase 4: Hazard Identification
Phase 5: HRC Handling Training
Phase 6: Sustainability Indicators
Phase 7: Mixed Battery Inspection
Phase 8: Decision-Making (Recycle / Dismantle / Discard)
Phase 9: HRC Sorting Operations
Phase 10: Final Workflow Assessment
Phase 11: Summary & LO Mapping

7. Didactical Materials
• Infographics: battery types, hazard cues
• Videos: HRC examples, battery degradation
• Panels: sustainability scores, safety warnings
• Voiceovers: guidance and error correction
• Interaction UI: gripper controls, decision buttons, inspection tools

8. Assessment Strategy
• Real-time hazard identification scoring
• HRC compliance (safe distances, resets)
• Battery handling accuracy
• Sorting decision correctness
• Sustainability impact evaluation
• Final integrated performance report

9. Expected Outcomes
At the end of the training, learners should be able to:
• Safely collaborate with an industrial robot
• Identify battery hazards and classify battery types
• Perform robot-assisted battery handling
• Make correct, sustainability-informed decisions
• Execute a full workflow independently
• Understand safety, operation, and maintenance concepts

10. Decision Conditions

RECYCLE – CONDITIONS
Structural:
- No acid leak
- No significant swelling
- No extensive burns
- Corrosion: Low or Medium only
Integrity:
- Outer case intact or minor cosmetic damage
- Shape and weight acceptable for robotic handling
Sustainability:
- Material recoverable ≥ 60%
- CO2 savings from recycling > CO2 cost of dismantling/disposal
Summary:
Battery is healthy or lightly damaged and safe to recycle.

DISMANTLE – CONDITIONS
Structural:
- 1–2 moderate defects (e.g., High corrosion, small burns, limited deformation)
Safety:
- No leak OR only minor leak
- Swelling = Moderate
Sustainability:
- Material recoverable ≥ 30%
- Valuable components present (metals, modules)
Summary:
Battery has damage preventing direct recycling but retains valuable internal components.

DISCARD – CONDITIONS
Critical damage:
- Acid leak = TRUE
- Swelling = High
- Burn damage = Extensive
- Corrosion = Severe
Risk:
- Structure compromised
- High risk during robot grasping or human–robot collaboration
Sustainability:
- Material recoverable < 30%
- Recycling/dismantling impact > environmental benefit
Summary:
Battery is dangerous or irrecoverable and must be discarded.

11. Script

[Full scripted steps for Phases 1–11 exactly as provided, including all system actions, user actions, conditions, and dialogues, preserved in this text field for structured storage and processing.]
